humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark includes a high percentage of humanities and arts topics (e.g., Art, Music, History, Media) treated purely as a source of complex, multi-constraint factual data. It leverages these domains to construct highly obscure, reverse-engineered factual retrieval items to test agentic search capabilities, rather than evaluating interpretive humanistic methodology.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: The benchmark consists entirely of short-form text natural language questions and textual reference answers, requiring agents to navigate text-based search queries and web pages.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Tasks require agents to resolve complex, multi-constraint queries (e.g., matching a fictional character to specific backstory criteria) by dynamically searching, parsing multi-hop constraints, and cross-referencing information across the open web to locate a single, verifiable short-string answer.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed as a general-purpose capability benchmark for web-browsing agents across multiple knowledge domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates frontier generative models and reasoning/agentic systems including GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, OpenAI o1, and OpenAI Deep Research.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being benchmarked are LLMs and LLM-based web-browsing agents.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes substantial factual content from History, Visual Arts and Performing Arts (TV, movies, art), Music, and Literature (fictional characters).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses exact match accuracy (graded by an LLM-as-a-judge) and confidence calibration error.

english_language.note: The paper and benchmark dataset are in English.

summary: BrowseComp is a general-purpose benchmark comprising 1,266 challenging, short-answer questions designed to evaluate the capability of web browsing agents to persistently search and retrieve obscure, deeply entangled information. The dataset features 'inverted questions' that are extremely difficult for humans or standard LLMs to solve but simple to verify automatically. The evaluation demonstrates that while standard LLMs fail near-completely, agentic systems utilizing search and reinforcement learning (like OpenAI Deep Research) can achieve high accuracy that scales with test-time compute.
key_findings: Standard models without browsing fail on this obscure fact retrieval, with GPT-4o achieving 0.6% and GPT-4.5 0.9% accuracy. OpenAI o1 achieves 9.9% utilizing internal reasoning alone. OpenAI Deep Research achieves 51.5% accuracy, which scales smoothly with increased test-time compute, reaching over 70% accuracy when utilizing parallel sampling and a best-of-N voting scheme. In contrast, human trainers solved only 29.2% of the tasks within a two-hour limit.
review_notes: Minor disagreements in naming the analytical tasks ('fact retrieval' vs 'information retrieval', 'multi-constraint search' vs 'constraint satisfaction'). Merged both sets of terms to include fact retrieval, multi-hop reasoning, constraint satisfaction, and fact verification. Harmonized the primary evaluation setting to 'zero-shot' encompassing the agentic browsing aspect.
